title: 2026 02 19 Petitions
displayTitle: 2026-02-19 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Bob Robertson: Require licensed premises to display drink prices, sizes, and alcohol content
topic: Petition of Bob Robertson: Require licensed premises to display drink prices, sizes, and alcohol content
description: Petition of Bob Robertson: Require licensed premises to display drink prices, sizes, and alcohol content Bob Robertson Ministry of Justice Patrick Takurua, Policy Advisor, Victims and Harm Reduction Lison Harris, Policy Manager, Victims and Harm Reduction Clubs New Zealand Lucy Waterreus, CEO
descriptionSummary: Petition of Bob Robertson: Require licensed premises to display drink prices, sizes, and alcohol content. Bob Robertson. Ministry of Justice

chunks.text: Ground green MP based in the Right. So, um, Roberton, we have 10 minutes for you. Take your submission as read, but I'll speak to it and the 10 minutes is yours and you might like to leave time for questions. Thank you. Uh, so thank you Mr. O'Connor, members of the petitions, uh, select committee for the opportunity for me to speak and respond to the submissions against my petition from Hospitality New Zealand and clubs New Zealand.

My name is Bob Robertson. I'm the petition author for requiring license premises to display drink prices, sizes, and alcohol content. I'm simply a consumer. Before I begin the detail, I want to explain why this petition matters. New Zealanders increasingly want and expect to make clearer more responsible choices about what they drink.

Social drinking habits are changing more New Zealanders are moderating their intake. Low and no alcohol options are a fast growing part of the market, and people are more conscious about what's in their glass. But while a supermarket bottle shows you exactly how much alcohol contains that clarity disappears.

The moment you step into a bar, you can't always tell if the pint you've ordered is 4 25 milliliters or 568 milliliters, or if a beer you've selected is 4% or 7%, or indeed how much it costs information, genuine informed choice isn't possible.

So my petition is simple targeted requests for transparency. It asks for consistency by having all unlicensed premises such as pubs, bars, and clubs clearly display for draft beer, cider, and wine sold by the glass. Four pieces of key information, the drink name, the serving size, the alcohol by volume, and the GST inclusive price.

This would be a small meaningful step ensuring everyone, staff included, knows exactly what's being poured at what strength and at what cost. Currently, practices vary widely. Some venues do display prices, others don't serving. They don't serving.

Sizes differ and many staff don't know the A BV of what they're selling. That's the alcohol by volume. The Ministry of Justice has even confirmed that this information is not always displayed for alcohol served on premise. Staff shouldn't have to guess and customers shouldn't have to ask repeatedly for the same basic information.

A clear display makes things easier and fair for both sides. The practicalities are straightforward. Venues already know the details, they just need to display them. Clearly. It could be on a chalkboard printed list or a digital menu. Updated whenever products or prices change.

The Ministry of Justice has confirmed that one, alcohol content can already be required under existing regulation making powers, and two, displaying price and volume could be added easily through a short amendment to Section 4 0 1 A of the sale and supply of Alcohol Act 2012.

This is incremental rather than disruptive. It touches a limited set of products, uses information venues already have, can be displayed using the boards and menus they already use, and can be checked alongside existing licensing inspections. It fits the pattern of small practical improvements.

New Zealand tends to favor now to the industry responses. So hospitality New Zealand acknowledges the petition, but argues legislation is unnecessary because in its words, most venues already do this in some, some shape or form. Clubs NZ goes further and characterizes the petition as unnecessary, impractical and not clearly linked to proven harm minimization outcomes.

Relying heavily on staff customer interaction as the primary way information is provided. They contend that information on a BV size and price is readily ready, readily available via trained staff. And that additional regulation would simply add cost complexity and red tape.

In my experience as a consumer, many venues happily provide signage providing the information I'm requesting. So additional cost complexity and red tape doesn't appear to be an issue for them. Where a venue doesn't provide signage, my experience is that bar staff often don't know the A BV or the serving size.

This directly challenges the assumption that staff interaction reliably, reliably provides accurate information and underscores my point, my core points. That information should be transparently available without needing to rely on staff knowledge or initiative. The industry lobby group's insistence on a strict harm minimization test also overlooks drink driving risk.

If a customer unintentionally consumes more alcohol than they realize because key information wasn't displayed, there are obvious safety and legal implications. At a minimum, it raises a fairness question, is it reasonable to place the entire responsibility on the customer when the venue chose not to make basic volume and a BV information visible?

My intention is to assist the committee by clarifying that this is a targeted low burden transparency measure designed to address clear and widespread information, gaps and inconsistencies with real harm minimization benefits At its heart, the petition rests on a clear principle transparency and the ability to make an informed choice of basic consumer rights.

In almost every other consumer context, new Zealanders are entitled to clear upfront information about what they are buying on-premise. Alcoholic drinks should be, should not be an exception. Both hospitality New Zealand and clubs New Zealand emphasize that information is available through menus, chalkboards and staff.

But these practices aren't standardized and vary from venue to venue. There's no consistent reliable way to know the actual core size, the price or a BV for draft products and wine sold sold by the glass. Some venues list prices, but not volumes. Some list names without a BV requiring customers to ask every time introduces friction and assumes a uniform level of staff training and product knowledge that is neither required by law nor achieved consistently across the sector.

A basic legible tariff for AFI set of products, compliments host responsibility by giving staff and customers a shared accurate reference point. Something many establishments already choose to provide clubs. NZ contends that any additional regulations be clearly linked to proven harm minimization outcomes.

And that my proposal doesn't meet that test. My petition does explicitly note that visible A BVN serving site information can support more mindful consumption and help people better track their intake, which will help drivers avoiding drinking above the legal limit. The primary rationale is consumer information and transparency, enabling informed choice in almost every other consumer context, packaged food and drink retail unit pricing.

New Zealand law already recognizes that people are entitled to clear upfront information about what they are buying. Applying the same principle to an on-premise pause is a modest extension of existing consumer protection norms. It shouldn't be necessary for consumers to guess whether a pint is 4 25 mil or 5 68 mil, or to rely on memory and staffed recall to understand whether one tap beer is substantially stronger than another, even if the committee were to place less weight on the harm reduction benefits.

The case for basic standardized transparency in this narrow area remains strong. So let me turn to the practicality and compliance burden. Both industry submissions characterize my position as impractical and burden burdensome. We've heard that before when New Zealand introduced a smoking ban in bars and restaurants, similar worries were raised that it couldn't be enforced and it would harm business.

Today that change is recognized as one of the most beneficial professionalizing steps ever taken for hospitality. Transparency and professionalism usually proved good for business two. The petition file requires only four data points per item. The name, the serving size, the A BV and the price.

Using flexible formats that venues already commonly employ chalkboards, printed lists, posters, or digital screens. The information itself is neither complex nor novel. Venues already know what they're pouring beside what size glass they're using, what they charge, and they receive AB information from suppliers.

The petition simply asks that they display information they already possess in a clear and consistent way, way. Club NZ points out that a BV volume and other details are already required on bottles and cans, and that responsibility for accuracy properly sits with the producer. My petition is entirely consistent with that framework.

It doesn't seek to move responsibility from for, for determining a BV to venues, but to ensure that when the same product is served on tap or by the glass, the essential information consumers take for granted in in its package, package form is not lost. Similarly, in the petition doesn't claim that United Kingdom measures can be lift lifted wholesale into New Zealand.

Rather it cites them to demonstrate that requiring clear display of serving size and a BV for common POS is both workable and accepted in comparable jurisdictions. So let me clarify some alleged ambiguities. Uh, Mr. Roberton, just where you, your 10 minutes is now very close to being asked.

Got, if you wanted to leave time for questions, I'm just, I'm just giving you that. Sure, sure. I, I, I'll, I didn't let, I'll Speed up. So, clubs NZ raises concerns about terms such as new, new normal viewing distance, manageable number of items and updates in a timely manner, suggesting these would create enforcement difficulties.

These are common concepts in existing regulatory practice and can be clarified in regulations for guidance. For example, new normal viewing distance can be aligned with existing signage requirements. The select committee may also note that the petition intentionally avoids over-prescribing formats precisely to give venues flexibility.

The requirement is outcome focus information should be legible and conspicuous at the point of decision for the relevant products rather than prescriptive. In conclusion, taken together, the industry submissions confirm an important point. There is currently no national requirement or standard that ensures that disinformation is displayed in a consistent way.

In practice. Approaches differ widely between venues and license types. My petition proposes a practical remedy that lines on-premise service of common pause with existing expectations for packaged goods using tools and formats already familiar to the sector. It enhances consumer choice, supports more mindful consumption in line with changing social drinking trend trends and reduces reliance on variable staff knowledge while imposing only at modest and proportionate compliance requirement.

And for these reasons, the committee is respectfully invited to view the petition not as an onerous new regulatory layer, but as a targeted common sense transparency measure that addresses clear information gaps and inconsistencies in the current environment.
